tagged_story_for_tts:
Stored effort.<[silence:600ms]> Silent trade.<[silence:600ms]>

The word money comes from the Latin moneta—the nickname for the Roman goddess Juno, near whose temple coins were first minted.<[silence:600ms]> Money turns work into portable trust:<[silence:200ms]> a farmer’s wheat becomes metal discs or digital numbers, letting them pay a blacksmith without hauling grain.<[silence:600ms]>

Before money, trade relied on coincidence—you needed someone who wanted your sheep and had the salt you craved.<[silence:600ms]> Coins fixed this by becoming a universal "I owe you," backed first by gold’s rarity, then by governments’ promises.<[silence:600ms]> Today, 90% of money is just data:<[silence:200ms]> when you tap a card, banks shuffle numbers in ledgers, no physical cash moving.<[silence:600ms]>

Money’s power lies in its precision.<[silence:600ms]> A $20 bill buys the same loaf of bread in New York or Nairobi because its value is agreed upon, not guessed.<[silence:600ms]> Yet it’s also fragile:<[silence:200ms]> if trust cracks (like in Zimbabwe’s 2008 hyperinflation), paper notes become worthless faster than they burn.<[silence:600ms]>

Three related subjects are barter’s hidden costs, how banks create money, and why shells once paid debts.<[silence:600ms]>
clean_story:
Stored effort. Silent trade.

The word money comes from the Latin moneta—the nickname for the Roman goddess Juno, near whose temple coins were first minted. Money turns work into portable trust: a farmer’s wheat becomes metal discs or digital numbers, letting them pay a blacksmith without hauling grain.

Before money, trade relied on coincidence—you needed someone who wanted your sheep and had the salt you craved. Coins fixed this by becoming a universal "I owe you," backed first by gold’s rarity, then by governments’ promises. Today, 90% of money is just data: when you tap a card, banks shuffle numbers in ledgers, no physical cash moving.

Money’s power lies in its precision. A $20 bill buys the same loaf of bread in New York or Nairobi because its value is agreed upon, not guessed. Yet it’s also fragile: if trust cracks (like in Zimbabwe’s 2008 hyperinflation), paper notes become worthless faster than they burn.

Three related subjects are barter’s hidden costs, how banks create money, and why shells once paid debts.
